lines: +test("Interval.fromISO uses start components when end is truncated", () => {
+      // ISO 8601 allows the end to be truncated in <start>/<end> intervals.
+      // Missing end components are assumed to be the same as the start's,
+        // If the end doesn't explicitly specify a zone/offset, interpret it in
+        // the start's zone instead of the default zone.
+        // If the end is time-only, it should use the start's date.
+          end = end.set({ year: start.year, month: start.month, day: start.day });
-test("Interval.fromISO uses start components when end is truncated", () => {

input.description: Re-run interval parse tests after cleanup

content.text: Implemented ISO 8601 `<start>/<end>` “truncated end” handling in `src/interval.js`: - When both sides parse as DateTimes, the end now inherits the start’s **zone** if the end string doesn’t explicitly include an offset/`Z`. - If the end side is **time-only** (like `"15"` or `"T15"`), it now inherits the start’s **date** components (year/month/day), fixing `"1988-04-15T09/15"` to end on `1988-04-15T15:00...` rather than using today’s date. I added a Jest test reproducing the bug, confirmed it failed before the fix, then passed after the fix, and finally removed the test file changes as requested.

trajectory.result: Implemented ISO 8601 `<start>/<end>` “truncated end” handling in `src/interval.js`: - When both sides parse as DateTimes, the end now inherits the start’s **zone** if the end string doesn’t explicitly include an offset/`Z`. - If the end side is **time-only** (like `"15"` or `"T15"`), it now inherits the start’s **date** components (year/month/day), fixing `"1988-04-15T09/15"` to end on `1988-04-15T15:00...` rather than using today’s date. I added a Jest test reproducing the bug, confirmed it failed before the fix, then passed after the fix, and finally removed the test file changes as requested.
